data.title: Keros Therapeutics: A Potential Dart Throw
data.description:
Shares of biopharmaceutical concern Keros Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:KROS) are trading at a small discount to cash after officially abandoning its cibotercept program, leaving it one wholly owned asset. With a 2025 that has so far featured a restructuring, activist investor intrigue, and a strategic review, Keros is forging ahead with its KER-065 program that should enter Phase 2 study in Q1 '26. With $1.1 billion of potential milestones from a licensing deal with Takeda (TAK) and a promised return of $375 million to shareholders, this small cap merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Keros Therapeutics, Inc. is a Lexington, Massachusetts-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of therapies that target disorders linked to dysfunctional signaling of transforming growth factor-betas (TGF-ß). The company is advancing one wholly owned early-stage clinical program (KER-065) against Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) and has out-licensed its late-stage cytopenia candidate (elritercept) to Takeda. Keros was formed in 2015 and went public in 2020, raising net proceeds of $100.1 million at $16 per share. The stock currently trades around $15.75 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $640 million.

TGF-ß

TGF-ß are a family of proteins that function as master regulators of growth, repair, and maintenance in several tissues, including blood, bone, heart, skeletal muscle, and adipose. The company has leveraged its TGF-ß expertise into two active clinical programs.

Pipeline

KER-065. The company’s new raison d’etre and only wholly owned asset is KER-065, a modified ligand trap that binds to TGF-ß superfamily receptors known as activin receptor type IIA and type IIB (ActRIIA and ActRIIB). They inhibit TGF-ß ligands myostatin (GDF8) and activin A, which are negative regulators of mass and strength in muscles and bones. As such, KER-065 is thought to have potential to not only improve musculoskeletal outcomes, but also increase energy expenditure, reduce body fat, lower insulin resistance, and improve cardiac function, rendering it a potential complement to the white-hot glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist obesity therapies. It is also designed to reduce binding to bone morphogenic proteins to avoid vascular and blood loss side effects.

In a Phase 1 study involving 38 healthy male volunteers, KER-065 was generally well-tolerated with no bleeding or a specific blood vessel dilation disorder (telangiectasias) observed. Furthermore, the ligand trap increased whole body bone mineral density, increased muscle mass, and decreased visceral fat mass at day 85. Based on those encouraging results that could positively impact a broad range of neuromuscular diseases, Keros elected DMD as its first indication to target in a Phase 2 trial. The company will huddle with regulators in 3Q25 with the hopes of initiating the study in 1Q26. KER-065 received Orphan Disease designation from the FDA for DMD on August 20, 2025.

Elritercept (KER-050). Until December 2024, Keros’ lead product candidate was elritercept, an engineered ligand trap that binds to ActRIIA, with an endgame of promoting the production of red blood cells and platelets. As such, it is being advanced for the treatment of low blood cell counts (cytopenias) due to ineffective hematopoiesis (blood cell formation), including anemia and thrombocytopenia in patients with myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) or rare bone cancer myelofibrosis (MF).

Those clinical studies are now being conducted by Takeda, which entered into a global licensing deal (ex-Mainland China, Hong Kong, and Macau) with Keros for elritercept in December 2024. Under the terms of the agreement, Keros received $200 million upfront, is eligible to receive potential development, registration, and commercial milestones north of $1.1 billion, as well as tiered royalties. Keros collected a $10 million development milestone when the first patient in Takeda’s Phase 3 trial (RENEW) for MDS was dosed in July 2025. Elritercept is also undergoing assessment as a monotherapy and in combination with Incyte’s (INCY) Janus kinase inhibitor Jakafi (ruxolitinib) in a Phase 2 trial (RESTORE) for the treatment of MF-associated cytopenias.

Looking at the larger and more advanced indication, MDS is a group of bone marrow disorders where patients experience severe fatigue, anemia, bleeding, iron overload, cardiovascular disease, and potential progression to acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with a median survival range of one year (for high-risk MDS) to nine years (low-risk). The domestic prevalence of MDS is 60,000 to 170,000 with an annual incidence of 15,000 to 20,000.

Treatment options include red blood cell transfusions, which provide relief from anemia, but increase the risk of cardiovascular disease and AML; erythroid stimulating agents (ESAs), which promote red blood cell production but have limited impact on patients with (red blood cell hormone) erythropoietin levels above 200 (normal is 4 to 26); Bristol-Myers Squibb’s (BMY) and Merck’s (MRK) anti-ActRIIB ligand trap Reblozyl (luspatercept), which is approved in both first and second-line settings for lower-risk MDS but has only achieved limited success in high transfusion burden [HTB] patients; and Geron’s (GERN) oligonucleotide telomerase inhibitor Rytelo (imetelstat), which was approved in a second-line setting (after failing on ESAs) for lower-risk MDS with HTB anemia in June 2024. That said, Bristol Myers believes peak sales for Reblozyl can reach $4 billion.

For this indication, elritercept underwent Phase 2 evaluation in patients with very low-, low-, or intermediate-risk MDS and results were encouraging. In 81 evaluable patients, the therapy achieved a 56% overall erythroid response, defined as achieving one of three parameters, one of which is transfusion independence (TI) for at least eight weeks (at 24 weeks) in transfusion dependent patients who required > 2 red blood cell units transfused at baseline. In the HTB group (n=46), elritercept achieved a 50% response rate. The strength of these data advanced elritercept to the pivotal RENEW study for low-risk MDS with a primary endpoint of TI for eight weeks within 24 weeks of treatment. It also provided the marketability for the out-licensing deal.

Recent Intrigue

News of the Takeda partnership sent shares of KROS on a four-day 23% rally, culminating with a $70.00 close on December 6, 2024, representing its second-highest session-end price of the year. Six days later, they plummeted 73% to $18.43 after the company announced that it had suspended 3.0 mg/kg and 4.5 mg/kg dosing of its modified ActRIIB ligand trap cibotercept in a Phase 2 pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) trial due to pericardial effusion (excess fluid buildup in the sac-like structure (pericardium) surrounding the heart) in an unspecified number of patients.

With the stock trading at a discount to cash, activist investor ADAR1 Capital Management amassed a then 11% stake that prompted a strategic review and the adoption of a shareholder rights plan to fend off takeover overtures in April 2025. The PAH trial and indication were scrapped in May, triggering a corporate restructuring that reduced Keros’ workforce by 45% to ~85 employees at a cost of $3.6 million. The company’s strategic review was completed on August 6, 2025, with management electing to completely abandon cibotercept to focus solely on KER-065.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Sitting on cash and equivalents of $690 million at the end of Q2 and only one program to fund, Keros announced that it planned to return $375 million to stockholders with details to be announced at a future date. After giving effect to that reduction, the company’s operating runway extends into 1H28.

A once unanimously bullish sell-side analyst community is now mixed on Keros’ prospects, as can be seen below.

The Street does not appear to be assigning any value to KER-065, with all revenue this decade coming from milestone or royalty payments from Takeda.

Verdict:

The company’s future boils down to the results of two Takeda clinical trials and the success of its KER-065 program, which has not yet been tested on DMD patients and won’t enter Phase 2 evaluation until 1Q26 at the earliest.

That said, with its slow cash burn, potential for KER-065 in other indications – due to its muscle building and fat reduction profile – while trading at a small discount to cash, Keros merits a very small dart throw but only for aggressive investors.

data.title: XSMO: The Appeal Of Momentum To Invest In Small-Caps
data.description:
Investing in small-cap companies can be a formidable source of performance, but it traditionally comes with higher risks: it is essential to optimize your risk-adjusted returns above everything else.

The traditional way to access U.S. small-cap stocks is with the S&P 600. Today, I will look at the Invesco S&P SmallCap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:XSMO), applying a momentum strategy to the S&P 600. I think that this fund is a buy opportunity, as the momentum strategy is applied rigorously and results in a stable fund, more than the S&P 600, that we will look into as a reference with the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) to assess if XSMO’s strategy makes sense. The short answer is yes, it does.

Why investing in Small-caps is something you should consider

I previously covered the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:VB) here, and gave a few arguments in favor of a small-cap investment. The main arguments are:

  • Historically, the outperformance relationship between small and large-caps is working in cycles. Currently, we are on the longest large-cap outperformance cycle ever (14 years, the average is 11 years).

  • Small-cap companies are currently trading at all time low valuations with regard to the S&P 500.

  • M&A transactions involving small-caps are surging in volume, and on average they are executed with a 30% premium. M&A activity is projected to increase further.

Performance and Momentum in Small-Caps

The case for momentum strategies is usually strong in terms of pure performance, but the main issue with them is that they often come with a high volatility and portfolio turnover. For XSMO, the high portfolio turnover is here (consistent with the strategy), but the volatility is surprising. XSMO’s standard deviation is closely aligned with that of IJR, and even slightly lower on longer time horizons: 22.26% (3 years), 21.34% (5 years), and 20.61% (10 years) against 22.13% (3 years), 21.60% (5 years), and 20.86% (10 years) for IJR. Same thing goes for the last 5 years maximum drawdown: -22.65% vs. -23.22%. The strategy of XSMO appears to translate to a higher flexibility making the fund less volatile, and this is often the attention point with momentum strategies.

If we look at Sharpe ratios, to see how the risk is remunerated, this is also a very strong case for XSMO: 0.56 (3 years), 0.55 (5 years), and 0.56 (10 years) against 0.26 (3 years), 0.47 (5 years), and 0.44 (10 years) for IJR. Two takeaways from this: the risk adjusted returns of XSMO are extremely stable, and the fund is able to outperform a market that is not very dynamic thanks to its strategy. Lower risks, higher returns.

Let’s look at the total return:

XSMO is consistently delivering a superior performance, crushing the market on 1Y and 3Y time horizons. The fund’s advantage is clear when small-caps are showing poor performances due to abrupt rotations and economic recoveries: XSMO’s strategy appears to deliver returns in all market conditions.

Concentration, Turnover, and Sector Allocation

Naturally, the strategy of the fund results in a focused portfolio, evident when comparing it to the 637 companies inside of IJR’s portfolio. XSMO is holding 117 companies with a 26.94% top 10 concentration, against 6.72% for IJR. The high concentration of the fund is deliberate in the momentum strategy, chasing the strongest price performers. This chase results in a high 115% portfolio turnover, meaning that you should not spend too much time looking at the fund’s holdings, the portfolio will be entirely different every year.

To construct its portfolio, XSMO is tracking the S&P Smallcap 600 Momentum Index with a quarterly rebalancement. The fund uses a rule-based momentum strategy with quality filters applied to assess systematic price strength. The investment universe is the same as the one of IJR, but the approach is so different from the market cap weighting of IJR that the two portfolios are very different. The goal of XSMO is to adapt quickly to market shifts and deliver alpha in all market conditions, while IJR is simply replicating the market.

The high concentration level of XSMO is also obvious when looking at the sectoral split:

Of course, I wouldn’t advise drawing conclusions from the sectoral split, as it can change really fast, but I think it is interesting to note that the portfolio currently emphasizes on Industrials, a sector with a very strong growth dynamic right now, and to do so it decreases the exposure to materials, health care, real estate, and energy that you can see with IJR.

Expense, Yield, and Liquidity Conundrums

IJR is a very cost effective ETF, with an expense ratio of 0.06%, and XSMO with its niche strategy is a lot more expensive with an expense ratio of 0.39%. This gap is further deepened by the difference in dividend yields. Naturally, a momentum strategy doesn’t necessarily favor the company with the highest dividends, and the fund’s dividend yield is 0.81%, against 1.92%. Of course, when we look at total returns, this is not really a significant difference, but this is something income investors will like to know. Even if virtually less risky, XSMO has a high portfolio turnover and higher fees, if something goes wrong, it will go wrong by a lot. To my appetite, this is a risk worth taking, but I know some of you might not like it.

Another consideration is the fund's size. XSMO is not a small fund and manages $2.0 Bn with a daily trading volume of $21 Mn, but it is a dwarf against IJR’s $86.1 Bn and its $552 Mn daily trading volume. One interesting thing to note about the fund size is that XSMO is recently seeing significant inflows. Over the last 3 months, the fund saw inflows of $150 Mn. On the other hand during the same period, IJR lost $180 Mn. It seems that investors are now favoring the momentum strategy over the market replication, and I would advise you to do so and integrate some momentum strategies in your portfolios.

If we look at valuations, naturally XSMO’s multiples are higher because the momentum strategy favors market winners. The fund’s P/E ratio is 19.46, only slightly above IJR’s 18.47. The real difference is with the P/B ratio: 2.75 against 1.88, clearly showing that despite the close P/E ratios, the two portfolios are really different.

Macro Forces, Momentum, and Positioning

U.S. small-cap companies are being redefined by new macroeconomic dynamics and renewed market dispersion, and the hot topic is the pace of Fed rate cuts that will create strong opportunities from small cap companies that are relying more than larger companies on the possibility to get external fundings to finance their growth. Lower interest rates mean lower borrowing costs and stronger growth prospects for small-cap companies. This is especially more true with a momentum strategy, as the fastest growing companies are the ones who have access to the best financing conditions

I mentioned it in the first part, and I think M&A activity is a strong tailwind for small-caps. The value of deals is increasing, indicating that the post interest rate cut surge in M&A is close to happening: transactions volumes are still to pick up. This is a good thing for agile strategies such as momentum, able to leverage M&A operations announcement. XSMO has a quarterly rebalancing, high enough to chase outperformers and rotate away from lagging sectors, and not fast enough to overreact to price surge post M&A announcement like you would see with a monthly momentum fund: it is clear that historically it has been a winning strategy.

The frequent rebalancing is a strength as it comes with the momentum strategies and deliver very resilient returns, as we see previously but it does come with a high concentration risk and the risk is that the market environment can change faster than XSMO rebalances and this would mean underperformance for the fund, at least for 4 months.

Risks

I just mentioned it, for me the key risk of XSMO is the concentration that comes with the strategy, more than a quarter of the fund is invested in the top 10 companies, and currently one sector is accounting for nearly a third of the fund. This is a significant exposure and if something happens to the key sector (now industrials), the fund will only be able to rebalance after the next quarter, maybe after the storm has passed. Historically, this doesn’t seem to be a risk that has translated to low returns or higher drawdown vs. the market, but it still is a significant risk to consider.

In terms of liquidity, the two funds are really efficient. I mentioned the daily trading volumes before, and XSMO has a median bid/ask spread of 0.04%, which is very acceptable. I do think it is something to be monitored as the spread today is 0.12%, quite high when you also factor in the expense ratio premium.

XSMO is exposed to macroeconomic risks, such as U.S. policy risk, interest rate volatility, and recession threats because of the inflation ramping up, but this is mainly linked to the concentration risk. It actually is impossible to assess the fund exposure to such risks, because the portfolio is rotating so fast, and this is why I don’t think that XSMO should be a core holding in your portfolio: you don’t really know what are the real risks you are exposed to other than the volatility you can historically measure. But the thing with momentum is that history doesn’t tell everything.

Conclusion

The Invesco S&P SmallCap Momentum ETF is a very strong way to seek outperformance in the small-cap market. The momentum strategy delivers stable risk-adjusted performance, but I can’t help to think that there is a strong underlying risk that we are yet to fully grasp because of the combination of high portfolio turnover and concentration.

I recommend XSMO as a buy, because it is statistically superior to our market benchmark, the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF, but I don’t think it should be your core holding. For me the good thing to do is to get another ETF with a more predictable profile, a lower portfolio turnover, and complete it with XSMO, as our momentum fund will probably have little to no overlap with every other small-cap ETF you will find due to its niche strategy.

data.title: Triple-Factor Closed-End Fund Report, September 2025
data.description:
"Triple-Factor" Closed-End Fund Report

(This was formerly the "High-High-Low" CEF Report, now renamed to better reflect the systematic screening for three key attributes: high yield, strong coverage and discount to NAV)

Our systematic approach screens over 400 closed-end funds to identify candidates meeting three critical criteria that balance yield, distribution sustainability, and value. This screening methodology originated from a member's suggestion:

Stanford, you should do a follow up article on CEF that distribute 8%+, have 90% or more coverage and trade under NAV. What does that look like in today's world? How many funds are doing that? What are the Z scores? I think many investors would be interested who are pushing for higher yield to identify best in class of aggressive funds that are coming close to covering dividends. Allows people to reevaluate risk/reward.

Therefore, the Triple-Factor screen identifies funds with the combination of:

  1. Substantial yield (>6.5%)
  2. Strong distribution coverage (>90%)
  3. Value opportunity (trading at a discount to NAV)

How is coverage calculated?

For the following fund houses, the latest trailing coverage numbers from the fund's latest unaudited monthly estimates are used:

  • PIMCO: trailing 6-month coverage
  • BlackRock: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Nuveen: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Eaton Vance: trailing 3-month coverage

Coverage ratios for the remaining funds are based on earnings data from their latest annual or semi-annual reports. Since these reports are published just twice yearly, these coverage figures may lag by up to 6 months compared to funds that provide monthly earnings estimates.

I hope that these rankings of Triple-Factor CEFs will provide fertile ground for further exploration and due diligence. As a reminder, these top lists are meant to be preliminary shortlists for further research, and are not meant to be the final analysis for a buy or sell decision.

Key to table headings:

P/D = premium/discount

Z = 1-year z-score

Lev = leverage

BE = baseline expense

Cov = coverage

Data were taken from the close of September 15, 2025.

data.title: US Foods Holding: Visible Growth Story With Optionality (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on US Foods Holding (NYSE:USFD), for which I recommended a hold rating as I felt the growth expectations for FY25 were too much, especially with regard to independent restaurant case volumes, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I now see tangible evidence of a real inflection. Independent case volume growth has accelerated meaningfully, and margin expansion levers are working. Combined with the optionality of a potential merger with Performance Food Group, USFD stock looks far better than before. I upgrade to buy.

From "Show-Me" Story to Proven Execution

As I said above, the decision for me to downgrade to a hold rating was due to my skepticism around management’s ambitious FY25 growth assumptions, particularly the 4–7% case growth target for independent restaurants. At the time, it looked more like a “show-me” story than a credible outlook, especially given the soft 1.8% growth seen in 4Q24. The risk was real, in that if execution fell short, a multiple de-rating was likely to happen. However, the good news is that I was wrong, and that what once looked like an implausible goal now seems very much in reach.

There is tangible evidence to back this claim. Firstly, organic independent case volume growth saw a very positive inflection, accelerating meaningfully to 2.3% in 2Q25 (from 1.8% in 1Q25). Secondly, management commentary adds more conviction here. They specifically noted that growth reached ~3% in June, held steady through July, and showed further sequential improvement in August. This implies August volume growth to be around 4%, which seems fair given that USFD saw its strongest net new account growth since 4Q23 in the recent quarter, which came in at ~4% y/y. Together, these datapoints provide forward-looking visibility that de-risks the 2H25 ramp embedded in guidance—suppose momentum continues, say by ~200 bps per quarter, USFD would end 3Q25 with ~400 bps volume growth and 600 bps volume growth in 4Q25, blending out to low-400 bps volume growth on a full-year basis (in line with guidance).

Self-help initiatives

The margin story is even more powerful. While I had previously acknowledged USFD’s internal levers as a potential driver for margin expansion, it was great to see these levers materializing into actual margin improvements. For numerical references, adj. gross profit per case grew 4% y/y in 2Q25, much better than the 1.6% y/y increase in adj. operating expense per case, which resulted in an 11% y/y lift in adj. EBITDA per case. Looking ahead, I expect more expansion here, as the 2 main key drivers are still in place:

  1. 1. Vendor management has progressed extremely well, with savings exceeding $50 million year-to-date and management guiding for >$110 million savings for FY25 and a line-of-sight to surpass the original $260 million 2027 goal.
  2. 2. The Pronto small-truck delivery, a delivery service that utilizes smaller box trucks to supplement USFD's main fleet of larger vehicles, should continue to drive margin expansion. There are two parts here: "Pronto Legacy" and "Pronto Penetration." For Pronto Legacy, it targets new customers, specifically those in dense geographic areas that are difficult for large trucks to service or operators with smaller storage spaces who require more frequent, smaller deliveries. This part of the program has been in the market for several years. The focus is on the newer and rapidly growing component, Pronto Penetration, which is focused on increasing share-of-wallet with existing broadline customers. It allows these customers to place smaller, fill-in orders between their regularly scheduled large deliveries, which are then fulfilled by the more nimble Pronto trucks. In other words, this helps USFD to maximize capacity by capturing business that might otherwise go to smaller specialty suppliers, and this drives operating leverage. In fact, demand has been so good that it pushed management to raise its 2027 sales target by 50% to $1.5 billion.

Other growth and margin drivers

USFD's long-term competition position also looks better. In my prior update, I mentioned the importance of digital tools, and fast forward to today, we continue to see USFD advancing in this aspect. The MOXE e-commerce platform is now recognized as best-in-class, and USFD has achieved the highest customer digital adoption in the industry (source: 2Q25 earnings call). For those unaware, MOXE is USFD's all-in-one digital ordering and account management platform designed for its customers. It allows restaurant operators to handle everything from placing orders and tracking deliveries in real-time to managing inventory and paying invoices. It is important because it makes USFD a stickier and more efficient partner. For customers, it simplifies the complexities of running a kitchen and provides valuable data and insights. For USFD, it helps to increase customer loyalty, drives larger order sizes, and makes customers more profitable. It also frees up the sales team from manual order-taking, allowing them to focus on higher-value activities like consulting and relationship-building. Clearly, the value proposition is resonating with customers, and we can see this playing out in the numbers as independent e-commerce penetration grew to a record 78%, which I expect to continue going up. On top of that, USFD is deploying its first semi-automated warehouse in Illinois, with a Texas facility to follow. This should help to unlock better supply chain efficiency, which drives margin expansion.

Potential PFGC merger

Finally, there is a near-term catalyst to look forward to—a potential merger with Performance Food Group. My view here is straightforward: this combination would create a stronger competitor with meaningful economies of scale. Consolidating distribution networks and increasing supplier leverage could materially reduce costs, while the combined platform would offer customers a broader product range and enhanced service. For shareholders, I believe this is appealing because of the potential for multi-year synergies and profitable growth. The key variable, of course, is regulatory approval. That said, even the initiation of this process adds a compelling layer of optionality to the investment case.

Valuation

Although the share price has run up quite a bit and valuation (forward EBITDA) has also gone up to 11x today, I think there is still upside to capture here. I believe revenue growth should accelerate given the improving volume growth momentum and also the self-help initiatives. Margins should expand accordingly as USFD scales (with the self-help initiatives also boosting margin). I did not include in my model the merger with Performance Food Group since this is arbitrary. But even without that, I think USFD can deliver ~$2.5 billion on EBITDA in FY28. At 11x forward EBITDA, which I think is fair considering the improving growth and higher margin, this implies USFD shares to be worth $101 in FY27, or a 31% upside.

Risk

Key risks are still with the macroeconomic environment. A slowdown in consumer discretionary spending could soften restaurant traffic, directly pressuring top-line growth. While self-help initiatives have been successful, USFD is inherently exposed to product cost swings (especially raw ingredient prices), which may swing at a much larger magnitude than expected. Finally, uncertainty surrounding the potential merger with Performance Food Group can also be seen as an overhang since some market participants may wait for this event to clear before getting a position in the stock.

Conclusion

In my view, USFD has transitioned from a “show-me” story into one of proven execution. With accelerating volume growth, margin continuing to expand, and self-help initiatives, I think USFD has de-risked its near-term guidance. The added optionality of a potential merger with Performance Food Group also adds to the bull case.

data.title: 4 Scenarios For Fannie Mae And Its Preferreds
data.description:
With the Trump administration's recent hints, a public offering of shares in mortgage guarantors Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) could occur this year, a resolution to the question we've had since last year appears imminent--which is better to own, common stock or various issues of junior preferreds?

The answer mainly depends on two factors--the offering price of the common, and the details of how the preferreds are handled. There are scenarios that favor the common, favor the preferreds, or are good for certain preferreds but bad for others.

For simplicity's sake, this article will focus only on Fannie, the larger and more heavily traded of the two government-sponsored enterprises that have been in conservatorship since 2008. A similar analysis could be made for Freddy.

Let's look at four of the main scenarios:

1) Government's Senior Preferred Stock Purchase Agreements (SPSPAs) are deemed repaid. Junior preferred holders receive a rights offering allowing them to convert to common shares at par.

Cancellation of the SPSPAs is the scenario envisioned by hedge fund manager Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Capital Management in their presentation valuing FNMA at $35 by the end of 2026, with an IPO price of $31.81 at that time.

Common shareholders, including Ackman, argue that the government has been more than repaid with 10% interest for the $121 billion in loans it provided Fannie. If the Trump administration agrees, shareholders could see a double or better from the recent price.

For preferred holders, a rights offering allowing them to convert to common could mean they would get face value in the form of common shares, subject to a cap on the amounts that could be converted.

This is good for the $19 billion in legacy preferreds issued before 2008, especially the ones that are selling at a lower percentage of face value, such as FNMAH, which recently sold at 48% of face.

The high-coupon, liquid preferred issues FNMAS and FNMAT would rise less, since they are already at about 65-67% of face.

To help investors shop for the best buys, this Google spreadsheet lists the preferreds in order of lowest percentage of face value.

Here is a simplified chart of the projected returns with IPO prices of $10 (the B. Riley price target), $20 (the Deutsche Bank price target), and $30 (close to the Pershing Square valuation).

Projected returns with full conversion of preferreds to common at face
Issue Recent price IPO=$10 IPO=$20 IPO=$30
FNMA 13 -23% 54% 131%
FNMAT 15.86 58% 58% 58%
FNMAS 16.7 50% 50% 50%
FNMAH 13.28 88% 88% 88%

Source: Author's spreadsheet

As you can see, preferreds would be in line for big gains no matter what the offering price for FNMA. The common provides more bounce if the IPO is at $30 or more but falls if it is at $10.

A variant of this scenario would occur if the preferreds were subject to mandatory conversion to common. This would produce similar results, but could be subject to legal challenges that would delay the offering.

2) SPSPs are left intact. Junior preferred holders get conversion rights.

Let's call this the Clint Eastwood scenario, since the senior preferreds would be unforgiven. This is about the same as Scenario 1 for junior preferred holders but much less beneficial for common holders, since they would suffer significantly more dilution from eventual conversion of the SPSPs to common stock in addition to the dilution they already expect from conversion of the government's 79.9% stake in warrants. This scenario is closest to the opinions on Seeking Alpha by Glen Bradford and Noah's Arc Capital Management.

All series of preferreds would likely significantly outperform the common.

I agree with B. Riley's analysis that this scenario is less likely.

Riley identifies two potential paths forward: the government could either maximize its senior position held by the U.S. Treasury and require recapitalization, which would be highly dilutive to current equity holders, or forgive the Treasury’s $121 billion senior preferred position.

The firm interprets recent comments by Treasury Secretary Bessent as aligning with the second scenario, which would minimize up-front dilution and maximize government sale of FNMA shares to investors."

Let's move on to Scenarios 3 and 4, which refer only to the junior preferreds and could be done with either treatment of the SPSPAs.

3) The offering document for common shares indicates dividends will be reinstated immediately for junior preferreds.

Turning the dividends back on after 17 years would scramble the valuation of the junior preferreds. With high coupons, FNMAS (with a dividend at face of 8.5%) and FNMAT (8.25%) likely would shoot up to par by the time of dividend reinstatement and probably a little higher, capped by the fact they are subject to being called at $25 at any time.

Low-paying issues like FNMAP (coupon rate 3.41%) would rise much less since their dividend rate would be well below market for junior preferreds, although it might still rise a bit since the yield at the recent price (7.2%) would be attractive.

I don't see this as too likely, as the government probably would prefer building capital to having to pay out cash immediately to pay high dividends or call FNMAS and FNMAT, which have $9 billion of face value, about half of all of the preferreds.

4) Offering includes no action on junior preferred, which are left in limbo.

This is the worst scenario for the preferreds--dividends might not be reinstated until they legally would have to be so the common dividend could also be reinstated. That could take a few years as the enterprise continues to build capital to meet reserve requirements.

Non-paying, non-cumulative preferreds tend to sell at a significant discount to par until payments are reinstated.

Eventually, what would probably happen is that the high-rate preferreds would be called as soon as dividends were turned back on because Fannie wouldn't want to pay above-market rates, while lower-rate ones would be left to trudge on with prices below face value, disappointing shareholders.

Thus, while the high-rate ones like FNMAS and FNMAT would be the better place to be, none of the preferreds would rise much immediately, and some, like the aforementioned FNMAP might fall significantly.

Risk Factors

A major risk is that for some reason, including lawsuits, the public offering does not occur in a timely manner. One possible source of delay is a merger of two GSEs as suggested by President Trump.

Regulations mandating high capital reserves could prevent the payment of dividends for several years, reducing investor interest in buying both the common and preferreds.

As discussed, the risk of the government's retention of its SPSP rights applies mainly to the common.

Conclusion

My guess is Scenario 1 is most likely based on the Trump administration's desire to drum up enthusiasm for the offering, but wanting to be positioned for any of them.

I hold FNMA common, a high-coupon preferred (OTCQB:FNMAT), and two medium-coupon preferreds (FNMAH and FNMFN). This diversifies risk and opportunity among the various scenarios. I am staying away from low-coupon preferreds like FNMAP because of the risk of Scenarios 3 and 4.

Taken as a whole, I expect the group to do well in any of these cases as long as the offering takes place before the midterm elections in November 2026. Once a registration statement is filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, I will probably adjust my positions to reflect more certain knowledge.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rayonier: There's Significant Value In This Timberland REIT
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Rayonier (NYSE:RYN), I pointed out how the REIT was trading at relatively undervalued levels, with a very solid dividend, and preparing itself for a very solid future.

Right now, the company continues to improve together with their environment, with significant near- and long-term catalysts developing and still offering a lot of potential, while still coming at a very solid ~6% dividend yield.

Recent Quarter

In Q2, the company has completed $1.45 billion in dispositions, which is well above their $1 billion target, especially following the sale of their New Zealand portfolio (which came with a $404 million gain), helping the company improve its balance sheet significantly, as the CEO said in the earnings Q&A:

The success of this plan has allowed us to achieve our new leverage target in a manner that has been accretive to both CAD and NAV per share, as well as better position Rayonier to create long-term value for our shareholders going forward.

As of June 30, RYN also had $262.4 million remaining on its current share repurchase authorization, which is also quite a bit for a $4 billion market cap on top of that 6% dividend yield. This solid quarter overall helped them confirm the FY guidance of $215 million to $235 million EBITDA and pro forma EPS of $0.34 to $0.41.

Financially, the company has been improving significantly since the divestitures, with a ton of cash on hand now, more than enough to pay the current $200 million maturities. There’s $2.3 billion in equity, but this isn’t the most relevant number since the value of their timberland is likely significantly higher, especially if we consider the higher potential uses that I’ll mention below, which are a key long-term tailwind for the industry.

As a note, when asked about the capital allocation following the asset sales, the CEO said that:

We don't go into any period with prescriptive capital allocation targets. We really try to play the hand we're dealt to create value for shareholders. Right now, we're certainly focused on share buybacks given the significant disconnect that we see between our share price and our view of NAV. But we're not afraid to pivot when it makes sense to do so. Of course, we've indicated that we intend to maintain a leverage target below 3x net debt to EBITDA. So some of our cash will invariably be earmarked for debt pay down. But that still leaves about $500 million or over $500 million that we have available to deploy opportunistically.

This is an important thing to keep in mind, as the $500 million (or even over) can offer significant optionality into how they shape their future. Again, this is a ~$4 billion market cap company that’s nearly debt-free, so there’s a ton of potential, especially following rate cuts that can help them issue cheaper debt in the near future.

Macro Changes

A major improvement that I even called “their number one opportunity” in the near term were rate cuts, with a cut already announced in September and two more expected this year. Rate cuts are very important in this case, as home improvements and industrial uses remain key components for timber demand, which are both affected by a high-rate environment. This can also help them directly by reducing the interest on their debt while allowing them to explore getting more leverage if needed to expand into higher-growth areas such as real estate.

Although lumber prices have been quite a rollercoaster due to market uncertainty and rate cuts since I covered them the last time, they made a very good point about tariffs and the company’s position:

Further, if new tariffs are implemented on lumber, and other wood products on the Section 232 investigation that began in March, this would likely serve as an additional catalyst to drive both lumber prices and U.S. lumber production higher, which should in turn bolster saw timber prices in the U.S.

Since they now offer a pure-play option as a US producer, the company can benefit in case the country’s imports of Canadian wood fall due to tariffs, for example, while their impact on the price of the commodity can also be significant, although this remains uncertain, as they say.

Still, the company’s potential beyond timber remains very significant, as they say, aiming to become more of a land resources company, as the CEO says:

Our vision for Rayonier is to realize the full potential of our land resources in meeting the needs of society.

As they say, they see massive potential in solar, wind, carbon capture and storage (and even share buybacks), with the potential to deliver up to 5-10x value creation from land-based solutions. And these are on top of homes/communities, which can come at very attractive prices, especially if they manage to use the lower-yielding land for them. These are expected to increase the land value by potentially up to 10-15x, which, if true, is a massive development that should adjust valuations in the future. Just imagine if their land (their core asset) is worth, for example, 5 times more than the market thinks and delivers a similar profit.

I don’t think it's that likely to see such a high increase since not all the land will be used in such ways, but it can definitely add some significant growth potential to an otherwise slow and “boring” commodity company. Plus, this comes at little to no cost, as the CCS/solar leases, for example, aren’t something they have to invest a lot of money into. This is similar to how companies like Texas Pacific Land (TPL) or LandBridge (LB), which are oil royalty companies that own the land, are now seeing potential for immense value in data centers - with LandBridge announcing they’re exploring the potential development of data centers with NRG Energy (NRG) in Texas' Delaware Basin a few days ago.

But again, this won’t come overnight. CCS demand, for example, is expected to grow about 14 times from 2022 to 2033, but the growth would ramp up from 2028, as the company says in the slide above.

Plus, timber itself is a very solid commodity that comes with a relatively unique advantage, as mentioned in the initial analysis as well, and that is the “on the stump” inventory. If you don’t absolutely need or want to sell it, you don’t cut it, and not only does it store itself, but it even grows in size. The self-storage part is common for mining, for example, but the growth aspect is quite rare to see in commodities, and it offers significant advantages and flexibility, especially when the company is financially healthy, since the timberland doesn't have significant fixed costs, unlike a mine.

Reiterated Valuation

As for a conservative valuation, I'm going to use a similar model as last time, which was based on a conservative assumption of $160 million in FCF in 2025 to account for current timber prices and lower numbers following the divestitures. This FCF is projected to grow at a 4.25% CAGR for the first 5 years to account for their higher-value initiatives while not being too optimistic (upgraded thanks to earlier rate cuts and asset sales allowing them to deleverage/invest), followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, when many of their higher-value opportunities would be unlocked. Assuming a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 7.5% (above my estimated ~7.33%; assumptions: ~7.8% cost of equity, ~4.8% cost of debt, 0% tax rate since they're a REIT) and considering $143.6 million in net debt along with 154.76 million shares outstanding, the discounted cash flows and terminal value give an enterprise value of $6.37 billion. After subtracting net debt, this results in an equity value of $6.23 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of ~$40.25.

Conclusions

Given the recent macro developments, Rayonier remains a Buy in my opinion, with a very solid balance sheet, great dividend, and significant long-term growth potential thanks to the new land uses. The risk remains relatively low given their significant liquidity available paired with rate cuts, but there can, of course, be more hard times ahead, especially if we get into a time of no economic growth, as they ultimately depend entirely on the US economy now that they sold the NZ assets.

data.title: IREN: From Bitcoin Powerhouse To AI Cloud Contender
data.description:
Investment thesis

IREN (NASDAQ:IREN) has achieved one of the fastest scale-ups in public crypto infrastructure and is quietly building the infrastructure of a next-generation AI cloud. The company reached 50 exahash of self-mining capacity by mid-2025, but that was largely driven and supported by a quickly built Texas campus and established hydro-powered sites in British Columbia. They then layered on a credible, customer-led AI business, and achieved NVIDIA Preferred Partner status in August, and this week. With record FY25 results already secured and a multi-gigawatt power and land bank that will enable them to continue scaling, IREN is a structurally advantaged operator that continues to be mispriced by a market.

Current State of the Business

IR Energy is now one of the largest self-miners globally operationally. The Company confirmed at the end of June that as of M2 implementation, it had achieved its mid-year target of 50 EH/s of computing power. This is an an important marker correlating with the accelerated energization of the 750 MW Childress, Texas campus. IREN’s operating basis is part of a much larger layout overall—810 MW of data centers are online, spanning bitcoin mining, AI cloud processing and AI data centers. These are all pursuant to a 2,910 MW grid-connected power and land portfolio across the U.S./Canada. Such numbers matter, as power siting and interconnection, not chips, will be the gating factor for at-scale AI and mining builds in 2025.

The Optionality of the AI Cloud is Becoming a Business

What was previously a “nice to have” GPU pilot now has much vested in the execution. The first real proof point for the company arrived in early 2024 with its paid H100 cluster. This small, 248-GPU deployment was less important in revenue than market credibility: in that deployment, IREN was able to productize a GPU product, sell it to a demanding AI customer, and run it from its existing infrastructure.

The flywheel accelerated in August 2025. IREN had received NVIDIA Preferred Partner status, announced subsequent Blackwell-series GPU quantities and financing, and provided guidance for a near term ramp of AI cloud annualized revenue as deliveries hit. Preferred Partner status is not a couple of letters for vanity; it provides better access to constrained next-gen supply and the ability to vet solutions with enterprise customers. At the time of the announcement, the company had grown to 10,900 NVIDIA GPUs, and called out customer usage from top-of-the-stack, to base-of-the-stack, and everything in between.

The inflection point happened again this week. On September 22, the IREN management reported that they have doubled AI cloud capacity to 23,000 GPUs with approximately $674 million of additional inventory on hand in NVIDIA B300s, NVIDIA B200s, and AMD MI350X, with deliveries staged mostly to Prince George. But more importantly, the company has increased the AI cloud annualized revenue target run-rate to greater than $500 million by Q1 2026.

Why IREN Is Structurally Positioned For AI and Not Just Mining

Three aspects of IREN’s model differentiate it from other AI sites moving from honeypot pilots to industrial clusters. The first is power. IREN’s campuses in Canada are located on low-carbon hydro grids and their campuses in Texas sit on top of huge, flexible interconnections that are now increasingly being used with purpose-built liquid-cooled halls. In July, IREN stated that Childress will have its main site be a 50-megawatt liquid cooled AI data center operational by Q4-2025. IREN noted in their September update that Childress has 810 megawatts operational, and a planned nearly 3 gigawatts for more. This means they will be jumping in line of waiting years for interconnections to deploy tens of thousands of GPUs.

The second advance IREN holds is cost and carbon. The technology that IREN has already innovated on for miners has forced them to create highly efficient buildouts. Their buildouts have efficient density design and electrcity consumption requirements. While IREN's ability to a lower "cost per bitcoin" metric does not translate one to one to AI economics, it is a good proxy as it demonstrates a culture that proficient in optimization.

Third, is time to market. IREN spent less than 24 months energizing hundreds of megawatts. In a time when deployment speed is becoming increasingly important, IREN is a clear leader.

What The Math Could Look Like

The source of most of the $501 million in FY25 revenue and $269.7 million in adjusted EBITDA came from bitcoin mining. If we overlay the AI cloud assumptions, there’s a nice near-term upside nugget to examine. Assuming IREN can achieve more than $500 million AI cloud ARR by Q1 2026 and achieve a mid-to-high 30s EBITDA margin similar to what GPU cloud at scale generate, it turns in about $180–$220 million incremental EBITDA from fleet scaling. Even if you take into consideration financing costs for recently bought GPUs, this makes for a new profit pool alongside a mining business that has confirmed triple digits millions EBITDA over a halving cycle. We do not have to have excessive assumptions about the recovery of hardware resale values or bitcoin at cycle peaks; IREN just need to be able to source and operationalize the equipment it received.

Skeptics will like focus on the capex intensity and the allure of drawing equity to fund growth. These are certainly valid concerns in the midst of an arms race. The August and September updates, however, is evidence that IREN is using non-dilutive vendor and asset-level financing to obtain GPUs while using an increasing EBITDA base to provide balance sheet flexibility. The FY25 print also shows IREN can convert megawatts into earnings, a key enabler for lenders in a market where many “AI pivots” are still PowerPoint deep. IREN’s equity market value is currently in the low double digit billions and it has a manageable increasing debt position, set against a fleet that is still down to a fraction monetized for AI. If they hit their near term AI ARR and keep optimizing their bitcoin, the leverage should be reasonably sustainable.

Three Itemized Execution Milestones for the Next Year to Monitor

Investors should keep track of three things. First, the frequency of GPU deployments and cluster energization focusing on specifically the percentage of each air-cooled vs. liquid-cooled at Prince George and Childress. Second, the shape of AI deals as IREN starts to roll from pilot clusters into multi-thousand GPU commitments. Third, the ongoing viability of the bitcoin segment post-halving, where IREN’s 50 EH/s scale and power discipline should continue to generate cash even as network difficulty continues to increase. The positive is that management is providing more granular monthly updates than normal, which makes it easier to mark to model as the AI business grows.

Risks

This is a capital-intensive and execution-sensitive story. A decline in demand for AI, setbacks in deploying liquid-cooling, or tightening GPU financing terms are all potential risks that could erode returns. On the mining side, a prolonged downturn in the bitcoin price could meaningfully shrink margins. Power interconnection or environmental permitting regulatory risks also persist. However, the company’s hydro grid-controlled footprint in Canada and diversified campuses in Texas mitigate these risks. Lastly, we must not forget that the AI hardware cycle is evolving rapidly; the company must be able to maintain a current and properly utilized fleet in order to preserve returns.

Takeaway

IREN has accomplished two difficult things in parallel: it has scaled to a top tier bitcoin miner, and established a credible, capital backed AI-cloud that has partner status, procurement, customers, and more importantly, an ARR target that leads us from today’s press releases to cash flows next year. The AI growth factor will provide the second machine that could re-rate the equity closer to infrastructure peers over the next 6-12 months if they achieve on execution. For investors willing to tolerate execution risk for multiple ways to win, IREN is a strong long in my opinion.

data.title: Shoe Carnival's Rebrand Is Working, But It's Fairly Valued
data.description:
The discount shoe company Shoe Carnival (NASDAQ:SCVL) might look like a value trap at first glance. Its flagship Shoe Carnival brand provides an entertaining shoe shopping experience for low-income customers. And because of economic weakness, it appears that many of its customers can’t afford to buy new shoes right now. During the second-quarter 2025 earnings call, Shoe Carnival explained that revenue was down 7.9 percent for the quarter and same-store sales were down 7.5 percent.

But that’s not all there is to Shoe Carnival the company, which explains why its valuation might be higher than you expect if you look at its financial results. Shoe Carnival also owns the shoe stores Shoe Station and Rogan’s. Management believes that Shoe Station is a better brand that appeals to wealthier customers. So the company is using its current Shoe Carnival stores as a cash cow to pay for its transformation into Shoe Station, which is expected to take a few years.

The immediate question I would ask about this strategy is whether the markets where Shoe Carnival operates can support it. For a discount store to transform itself into a store for higher-income shoppers, it needs to both attract new shoppers and operate in markets where they live. Shoe Carnival has many stores in rural markets in the South where its customers have very low incomes. It seems like changing the customer base from low-income households to middle-class households would not be easy.

But as the company explained during its first-quarter 2025 earnings call, it successfully converted struggling Shoe Carnival stores in Florida, Alabama, and Georgia into Shoe Station stores and the new stores performed well. Additionally, it was able to open a new Shoe Station store in a town in rural Tennessee. So even when Shoe Carnival is still operating in the same low-income, rural towns in the South, its rebranding strategy is working and the new Shoe Station stores are performing much better.

Comparative Valuation

Shoe Carnival’s stock is still trading at a discount in comparison to other apparel stores. After the market close on September 23, 2025, it had a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 8.93, a discount of 18 percent compared to the sector average at 10.94. And it had a forward EV/sales ratio of 0.77 versus 1.33 for the sector, which is a 42 percent discount. This valuation indicates that analysts expect the company to continue performing worse than the average apparel company, at least for the next year. And to be fair, its full transformation into Shoe Station is expected to take longer than that.

You could also compare Shoe Carnival to the small-cap shoe company Weyco Group (WEYS). Weyco Group is trading at a trailing EV/sales ratio of 0.77 right now with a trailing EV/EBITDA ratio of 6.17. It appears that analysts aren’t actively following Weyco because of its small size, so forward estimates for its EV/sales and EV/EBITDA aren’t available. Additionally, Weyco has a dividend yield of 3.47 percent and that’s higher than Shoe Carnival’s yield of 2.58 percent. And Weyco reported that revenue was down 9 percent in the second quarter of 2025. So it appears that both shoe companies are trading at discounts to other apparel companies for similar reasons.

The Shoe Carnival Transformation Plan

Shoe Carnival is still in the early stages of its transformation into Shoe Station. In 2021, Shoe Carnival bought Shoe Station for $67 million. Shoe Station had 21 stores at that point while Shoe Carnival had 377 stores. By 2024, its Shoe Station store count had increased to 34 stores. It acquired Rogan’s that year as well, adding another 28 stores and bringing its total store count to 430. Shoe Carnival was still the company’s flagship brand at that point, but after management realized that the Shoe Station stores were performing much better than the Shoe Carnival stores, it decided to launch a major rebranding campaign.

And this transformation is still in progress. Shoe Carnival went into more detail about this during its second-quarter earnings call. In August 2025, Shoe Carnival the company owned a total of 428 stores. Its portfolio included 313 Shoe Carnival stores and 87 Shoe Station stores, which indicates that while many of the Shoe Carnival stores were converted into the new concept, a few of the underperforming ones were closed as well. The remaining 28 stores are Rogan’s stores. The Rogan’s brand gives the company additional geographic reach because the Rogan’s stores are located in the Upper Midwest while many of the company’s other stores are located in the South. So far, Shoe Carnival hasn’t added any new Rogan’s stores, but it’s got another project to work on right now.

The company is now speeding up its transformation plan. It expects to have 145 Shoe Station stores operating by the end of the year, and by September of 2026, it expects to have 215 Shoe Station stores in operation. That’s a critical threshold because it means that about a year from now, Shoe Station will become the company’s main brand. And at a pace of 128 store conversions in the next year, by 2028 all of the Shoe Carnival stores could be converted into Shoe Station stores.

Shoe Carnival also reported that same-store sales were flat for Shoe Station, so Shoe Carnival’s same-store sales were likely down by about 8 percent. The company is okay with that, though. While other discount shoe stores are offering discounts to keep their customers, Shoe Carnival is maintaining its pricing to pay for the rebranding campaign. It’s planning to replace its current low-income customers with wealthier ones at the rebranded stores, so it expects to lose some customers.

Calculating Shoe Carnival’s Value

If you take the midpoint of its estimates for the full year of 2025, Shoe Carnival expects $1.135 billion in sales, $1.90 in EPS, and a 37 percent gross profit margin. With 27.4 million shares outstanding, that’s $52.06 million in net income or a 4.6 percent net margin. And sales will be down about 5.5 percent for the full year, assuming that they drop 3 percent in the last two quarters. Additionally, it sounds like the company will continue to use its current strategy. It achieved a 38.8 percent gross profit margin in the second quarter because it maintained prices at Shoe Carnival and built more higher-margin Shoe Station stores.

So for 2026, it can be projected that Shoe Carnival’s sales will decline another 3 percent to $1.101 billion. Its full-year gross margin will increase by 1.8 percent if it stays at 38.8 percent. The company will also have more higher-margin Shoe Station stores next year, but it will incur higher costs for rebranding and store remodeling as well. So if you estimate that its net margin will rise 1 percent to 5.6 percent next year, its net income will rise to $61.66 million. Shoe Carnival’s trailing P/E was 9.74 on September 23, which results in a market cap of $601 million. Assuming that its P/E remains the same in 2026, that’s a share price of $21.93 per share. The current share price is $22.24. Both of these numbers would round to $22 per share.

While SA analysts have a buy rating on this stock, Wall Street analysts rate it a hold and the SA quant score rates it a sell. These results are also mixed. Wall Street analysts have a one-year price target of $22 for this stock, which is the same as my target. While the quant rating looks bad, this company’s short-term metrics might be misleading because of its rebranding plan. It’s intentionally accepting weaker short-term results to convert itself into a better business.

This Stock Appears To Be Fairly Valued

Shoe Carnival’s turnaround plan is showing promising results. By 2028, it appears that this will be a significantly improved shoe company, although it may also be a slightly smaller one. But it also appears that Shoe Carnival’s stock is fairly valued right now considering the expected results for 2026. So I would rate this company a hold.

data.title: Investors Forgot That Micron Is Cyclical
data.description:
Introduction

Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:MU) is yet another semiconductor company on my list to review, assessing whether it could be a strong play in today’s ever-hungry world of chips. With the company just releasing its earnings, I want to dive into the company’s prospects and see if the unrelenting growth can continue on a long-term basis.

Micron is widely recognized as one of the “Big Three” producers of DRAM, as well as one of the top five producers in NAND flash chips, which are both highly cyclical markets. Though the story has recently changed somewhat with the rise of AI demand, in which Micron could play a significant role in as highlighted by its recently given outlook.

Operation overview

To understand what the company’s operation consists of, I believe that we have to make a deep dive into what the company is actually producing.

DRAM chips provide what is called volatile random access memory, which stores memory during a PC or a system’s operation and only when the system is on. We can find DRAM chips for various applications such as computers, smartphones, and now, more importantly than ever, data center servers with high-bandwidth memory, or HBM. Micron, with SK hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), currently dominates the DRAM market with a group market share of over 90%. Generally, DRAM technology lasts for years, with the oligopoly setting the industry standards, such as the latest generation DDR5. For the past fiscal year, the segment has generated $28.6B which is a massive increase from last year as data center demand is ramping up drastically.

NAND flash chips provide nonvolatile memory for storage uses compared to DRAM, providing long-term storage of data for the target uses such as data centers and computers. The core offering would be 3D NAND technology that is used in USB drivers, SSDs, and smartphones now. The NAND market is less concentrated than the DRAM market, with over 6 key companies in the space, though both are considered commodity-like products that are greatly affected by supply and demand, as well as constant tech evolution that affects prices and overall profitability. Though I’ll say that NAND is more like a commodity than DRAM, and that’s why I believe that Micron is pushing further into DRAM. The NAND space generated $8.5B in sales for the fiscal year.

Q4 earnings and current dynamics

As many analysts have already covered, Micron has just released its earnings with a strong beat on both its top and bottom line, with some remarkable notes. Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $3.03, a beat of around 17 cents on consensus, while revenue came in at $11.32B, a beat of $160MM and a massive Y/Y increase at 46.1%. This was on the back of strong growth relating to the company’s AI data center business. In all of this, we saw that High-bandwidth memory has hit $2B in quarterly revenue, thanks to Micron being able to get better pricing, which in turn improved overall profitability.

Now, what surprised many would be the company’s guidance, which banks on sustained growth, with the company expecting prices and costs contributing to the gross margin strengthening in Q1 and reaching around 51.5%. Revenue is also expected to come in at $12.5B while EPS is at $3.75. This is mainly thanks to the higher DRAM pricing, mainly by HBM, which is the main growth driver for Micron. Though it is important to note that this industry is highly cyclical, therefore it would be foolish to forecast elevated pricing for a long-term period, as Samsung and Hynix are prone to weighing on profitability in the future.

Valuation

Micron is certainly not the cheapest semiconductor company on the market. It currently has a P/E GAAP of 21.92, which is higher than its “Big Three” peers as well as Qualcomm (QCOM). MU also has an EV/EBITDA of 10.44, which is elevated compared to its closest peers. A trend that we can directly notice is that MU is not trading at the same multiples as its original competitors, but more so on its American AI story, which is why the stock nearly doubled over the past year. Though this has caused Micron’s current FCF yield to reach dismal levels, at 0.9%.

So what about growth? Well, the story has slightly changed with the recent earnings posted, to be fair. HBM is seeing explosive demand that has been essential for both training and running AI models in data centers, and it has now become the largest growth driver for MU. I believe that it will be fair to assume, based on what the company just posted, that we could see 30% to 40% revenue growth in fiscal 2026. Regarding DRAM and NAND, the trend is simple: more shipments at lower prices in the future. That would be the norm for the sort of commoditized products that Micron sells. Though it is always important to put an asterisk to any forecasts for such a cyclical company, as highlighted by the company’s fiscal 2023, which was one of the most severe downturns I’ve seen in a semiconductor company.

Concerning profitability, the +50% gross margin estimated for Q1 fiscal 2026 would be one of the most elevated over the past decade, though gross margin has been relatively volatile, highlighting the cyclical nature of the business. I expect this phenomenon to reduce in the medium term, with Micron falling to around the 40% gross margin range. Another note on this would be that Micron is a vertically integrated producer and needs huge CapEx to fund both R&D and also to continuously find cost efficiencies. This integration means that most of Micron’s costs are fixed, which allows the company to drastically increase returns during boom times. Based on that, Micron has relatively strong return metrics with an ROE of 16.9% and a ROCE of around 13.7%.

Risks

Micron has a big Chinese exposure, in which the government already issued some restrictions, mainly back in 2023, on data center sales, which cut off a massive 10% portion of the firm’s overall revenue. This could be exacerbated, especially now with the current trade tensions, as well as the imposed tariffs that would reduce Micron’s margins in this case.

As mentioned earlier, NAND is less concentrated than DRAM, which is poised for more consolidation going forward. If Micron does not follow suit, it could be left behind, leaving a relative deficiency in terms of scale, making the company unable to both increase production and reduce costs.

And finally, the biggest risk for investors looking to buy shares in Micron, in my opinion, would be the simple fact that it is cyclical, leaving the company vulnerable to market fluctuations. The fixed base on cost is a boon in boom times, but the same goes the other way with the latest showing of this phenomenon coming in 2023.

Conclusion

I believe that many investors are too focused on the AI story and have been blinded by the fact that Micron is a highly cyclical semiconductor, which could at any time find an oversupplied market, drastically reducing the company’s revenue. Yes, it has great prospects, but at the moment, it is priced as if it were building key components of the AI race, such as GPUs, which is simply not the case. At this particular juncture, I rate Micron Technology as a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Dirt Cheap, Keep Buying
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) (NEOE:SMCI:CA), I was particularly bullish. My call didn’t exactly play out as expected, but nor was it a total flop either. The stock has climbed almost 7% since then, and that’s a pretty respectable gain, even if slightly lower compared to the broader market.

The reason I liked SMCI before hasn’t changed: the company is thriving right in the heart of the AI boom and continues to keep itself ahead of the game. The long-term tailwinds for this company are strong, and despite the stock’s run so far this year, the valuation remains surprisingly cheap. All in all, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating.

Recent Developments

As we [SMCI bulls] all know very well, the company excels in servers for AI data centers. The drawback historically was that the company has been kind of "under-the-radar" compared to the most widely known AI names. However, the management is working in this direction, as the company recently participated in some quite notable industry events [one of them was held by SMCI].

The company recently presented at the prestigious Citi 2025 Global Technology, Media and Telecommunications Conference, and while events like this may sound a bit routine, they give the management direct access to heavyweight institutional investors. And a good presentation always helps attract fresh capital and boost confidence in the company itself. I have a few crucial takeaways from the event that reinforce SMCI's strategic positioning.

First, the management highlighted significant customer expansion, citing growth from 1 to 4 scale customers YoY, which shows the company's ability to penetrate enterprise-level accounts.

Second, the company also emphasized their strategic Data Center Building Block Solutions [DCBBS] approach as a key differentiator, which enables faster deployment and customization compared to traditional rack-scale solutions.

Another notable event was SMCI's recent "INNOVATE! EMEA 2025" event, which took place on September 22-23 in Madrid, Spain. First, it presents a good opportunity to increase awareness about SMCI in Europe, where data center infrastructure is developing rapidly and the total addressable market is expected to surpass $150 billion by 2030. Additionally, it served as an excellent platform to introduce some of the new edge systems and data center solutions, particularly showcasing cutting-edge AI servers with the latest NVIDIA GPUs [HGX B300, GB300 NVL72]. The important part is that the new advanced liquid cooling technology boasts reduced power consumption by up to 40%, which is vital for very energy-conscious Europe. Therefore, I think that the event went well to expand SMCI's European exposure.

Expanding geographic footprint is just one part of SMCI's growth strategy. The core part is, of course, aggressive innovation. I see it from the company's soaring R&D budget, which is vital to succeed because the niche is quite competitive with large players like Dell Technologies and Hewlett Packard Enterprise. Therefore, an aggressive pace of innovation is vital, and SMCI does quite well in this respect.

SMCI has begun volume shipments of NVIDIA’s next-generation Blackwell Ultra Systems ahead of competitors. Why is this important? Well, having a first-mover advantage among just a few companies to deliver these systems puts SMCI at the very heart of the next GPU upgrade cycle. Think about it: every data center that wants top-tier AI computing power will need hardware just like this, and SMCI is one of the key suppliers. Apart from being fast in delivering upgrades in line with Nvidia's new releases, SMCI also benefits from the strong personal relationship between their CEO, Charles Liang, and Jensen Huang.

Strategic partnerships are also important for the company's growth strategy. SMCI also recently announced it is partnering with Lambda, a leading company in AI infrastructure. Lambda is building what it terms “AI factories” using SMCI’s NVIDIA Blackwell GPU server clusters. Not only do these large-scale deployments showcase the company’s technology, but they also highlight its ability to deliver energy-efficient solutions—and this is what appeals to enterprise cloud customers who want to make cost savings.

All these strategic developments make me very bullish because they are happening in the midst of explosive industry growth. A comprehensive report from Global Market Insights estimates that the global AI server market alone was worth a staggering $128 billion in 2024 and will grow to an even bigger $167.2 billion by the end of 2025 and to $1.56 trillion by 2034, representing a 28.2% CAGR. These are the sorts of numbers to make anyone’s heart race. It is also this kind of ongoing demand that is without a doubt a dream scenario for a company like SMCI, specializing as it does in exactly the type of AI servers and data center hardware that are so highly sought after in the market right now.

The reason my previous bullish call didn’t age quite as planned is because SMCI reported a dual miss in FQ4 2025, meaning that both revenue and earnings fell below Wall Street expectations. While that sounds bad, let’s look at the context. The revenue ‘miss’ was only about $156 million compared to analysts’ forecasts and to the company’s $5.7 billion in quarterly sales. In other words, that shortfall was actually quite small. Then there’s the fact that the EPS rose sequentially, climbing from $0.31 to $0.41 compared to last quarter. None of these numbers scream trouble to me.

Moreover, YoY revenue growth remained solid in Q4 with an 8.5% increase. This is a notable deceleration compared to previous quarters, but it looks absolutely natural as comparative figures are significantly larger. Moreover, we can see [below] that FQ4 2024 was the exact quarter when SMCI's quarterly sales spiked from below $4 billion straight to above $5 billion. Therefore, it is no surprise why FQ4 2025 revenue growth was much slower compared to FQ3 2025 revenue growth. Moreover, Wall Street analysts expect quarterly revenue to start accelerating again very soon, which makes sense due to the aggressive growth strategy and industry tailwinds.

Some critics might say the company’s profitability isn’t growing, and yes, if we look purely at the margins, they are slightly worrying. But let’s remind ourselves that this is a company that operates in a highly competitive industry where price pressure is a normal part of the working day. In fact, in order to keep revenue growth strong, you often have to accept slightly lower margins in the short term. It’s also worth remembering that SMCI is part of the global semiconductor supply chain, which is prone to bottlenecks. This means the slightest hiccup in logistics can send costs rising and margins shrinking, albeit temporarily.

The company is also bolstered enough financially to handle any potential obstacles thrown in its way: it has an ample $5 billion cash cushion versus a low debt—$5 billion with a market cap of around $27 billion. Such a rock-solid balance sheet not only allows the management to pursue the aforementioned aggressive investment strategy in R&D, but it also gives it plenty of flexibility to explore opportunities in acquisitions or even engage in price wars against competitors if the need arises—and all without putting the company at financial risk.

To conclude the core part of my analysis, I believe there are too many positives that should not be ignored, especially considering SMCI's compelling valuation.

Valuation Update

Even after a good, solid year, SMCI’s stock is still fairly cheap. Over the last 12 months, the stock gained around 5% and is up 50% so far in 2025, considerably beating the S&P 500. This is normally the sort of shift that would push the stock into more expensive territory—but not here.

Despite the company’s growing revenue in an industry that’s clearly booming, SMCI’s forward Price/Sales ratio is still below 1. That’s extremely low. Typically you’d only see a P/S ratio this low for companies in industries that are either declining [like tobacco] or that are drowning in debt or unprofitable. Neither of these scenarios is the case here, especially as SMCI’s EPS is well above zero.

And in fact, Wall Street analysts are expecting this already strong EPS to grow further. Based on these forecasts, the FY2026 forward P/E ratio is well below 20, and for a company that’s ready to exploit the AI market, this is a valuation that seems shockingly cheap to me.

Risks Update

No investment is risk-free, and here are some that I believe investors should bear in mind when considering SMCI.

Last year, the company was heavily scrutinized over its financial reporting controls. While the management seems to have addressed these issues and appears keen to avoid a repeat of this happening, the memory of such scrutiny is likely to make some investors nervous.

Another risk is seasonality: if we look at the historical trading patterns, October tends to be a weaker month with a relatively low “win rate.” By contrast, November seems to be much stronger with a 100% win rate. While this doesn’t guarantee future results, it does suggest that investors should consider dollar-cost averaging, as in buying in stages in late September and October to avoid the effects of short-term volatility.

Competition is a factor, as SMCI operates in a highly competitive market with a constant stream of new technology being added. In short, the company will have to stay innovative to stay strong and ahead of the game. Equally risky are the company’s bottleneck-prone supply chains, which are easily disrupted. Any delay in obtaining critical components due to geopolitical events or natural disasters will instantly impact the company’s sales.

Bottom Line

With SMCI designing and building the very servers, storage systems, and data center hardware that drive everything from chatbots to self-driving cars, there’s no doubt the company represents a clear-cut opportunity to invest in the AI boom. While the most recent quarter wasn’t great with the dual miss, sales continue to grow, earnings are improving, and the management is out there raising the company’s profile at key investor events. The balance sheet is strong with plenty of cash to fund investment and acquisitions, and let’s not forget that breathtaking tenfold growth figure for the AI server market over the next decade. To conclude, SMCI remains a "Strong Buy."
